title: Mrouzia
subtitle: Moroccan honey-braised lamb with raisins and almonds
excerpt: Lamb braised for hours with ras el hanout, saffron, and ginger, then finished with honey and raisins until the sauce becomes thick, dark, and sweet. Almonds added at the end for crunch. The most heavily spiced and sweetest of the Moroccan tagines. Traditionally made at Eid al-Adha.
sourceNotes: Mrouzia is one of the oldest Moroccan tagines, associated specifically with Eid al-Adha and the tradition of sacrificial lamb. The combination of heavily spiced meat with honey, raisins, and almonds is a direct continuation of medieval Andalusian-Arab cooking in Morocco. Paula Wolfert includes a version in Couscous and Other Good Food from Morocco.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the day after. Reheat gently and add fresh toasted almonds.

content.text: Mrouzia requires a long, gentle cook — typically 2 to 2.5 hours. The lamb must be completely falling-tender before the honey and raisins go in. If the raisins are added while the meat is still tough, they will dissolve entirely by the time the meat is done.

The sauce should reduce to an almost jam-like consistency in the final stage: thick, glossy, and very fragrant. Watch it carefully once the honey is added — it can scorch quickly. The dish should be distinctly sweet but the spice complexity should counterbalance the sweetness. A dish that tastes only of honey is unbalanced.

Put the {{lamb-shoulder}}, {{ras-el-hanout}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, {{black-pepper}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, and saffron water in a heavy casserole. Add 400 ml of water. Bring to a boil, then reduce to the lowest possible simmer. Cover and cook for 1.5 to 2 hours until the lamb is completely tender and pulling from the bone.

Add the {{honey}} and {{raisins}} to the pot. Stir and cook over medium-low heat, uncovered, for 20 to 30 minutes until the liquid reduces to a thick, glossy sauce that coats the meat. Stir frequently in the last 10 minutes to prevent scorching. Meanwhile, toast the {{blanched-almonds}} in a dry pan until golden. Serve the mrouzia over couscous or with khobz, scattered generously with the toasted almonds.

items.symptom: Sauce scorching during the honey stage
items.cause: Heat too high once honey is added
items.fix: Reduce to the lowest heat and stir constantly in the final 10 minutes. The high sugar content makes the sauce prone to catching. A heavy-bottomed pot is important

items.cause: Too much honey or raisins very sweet
items.fix: Use only 2 tablespoons of honey and reduce raisins to 100 g. The ras el hanout and black pepper should provide enough counterbalance

items.cause: Cooked at too high a temperature
items.fix: Mrouzia needs a very gentle sustained heat. If the braise is bubbling actively, it is too hot — reduce until barely simmering

content.text: Mrouzia is the Eid al-Adha dish of Morocco. After the sheep is slaughtered and portioned, the shoulder goes into this tagine and the whole neighbourhood fills with its scent as it cooks through the morning. The dish was traditionally made in large quantities and preserved under its concentrated sauce for several days — the honey and spices acting as a natural preservative. It represents the peak of Moroccan sweet-savoury cooking, a flavour combination that has its roots in the medieval Arab culinary tradition that shaped Moroccan food from the 8th century onward.
